questions.stem: A data scientist instantiates `XGBClassifier(n_estimators=300, learning_rate=0.05)` in Snowpark ML. Compared to `XGBClassifier(n_estimators=100, learning_rate=0.15)`, what is expected when both are trained on the same data?

options.text: The 300-estimator model is faster to train and more likely to overfit
The 300-estimator model with lower learning rate typically generalizes better but takes 3x longer to train
Both models produce identical predictions because n_estimators × learning_rate is approximately equal
The 100-estimator model always generalizes better because fewer trees prevent overfitting

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: a smaller learning_rate (0.05) combined with more trees (300) is a standard XGBoost best practice. The smaller step size makes each tree contribute less, requiring more trees to achieve the same fit level, but generally produces a more generalizable model. Training time scales linearly with n_estimators, so 300 trees takes approximately 3x longer than 100 trees.

**A** is wrong: more estimators with lower learning rate typically reduces overfitting rather than increasing it.

**C** is wrong: n_estimators × learning_rate is not a conservation law; the models learn different weight distributions.

**D** is wrong: fewer trees with a high learning rate can overfit in fewer rounds; more trees with a lower rate is generally preferred.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index

questions.stem: A Snowpark ML Pipeline containing a StandardScaler and a LogisticRegression is trained with `pipeline.fit(train_df)`. A new batch of data arrives. What is the CORRECT way to preprocess AND predict in a single call?

options.text: pipeline.predict(new_batch_df) — the Pipeline applies all preprocessing steps before calling the final estimator's predict()
pipeline.transform(new_batch_df) then pipeline.predict(transformed_df)
scaler.transform(new_batch_df) then model.predict(scaled_df)
pipeline.fit(new_batch_df) then pipeline.predict(new_batch_df)

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: one of the core benefits of a Snowpark ML Pipeline is that calling `predict()` on the pipeline applies all upstream transformation steps (StandardScaler in this case) before passing the result to the final estimator. This is the whole point of the Pipeline abstraction — a single call handles the complete preprocessing and prediction workflow.

**B** is wrong: `Pipeline.transform()` applies only the transformers (not the final estimator), so calling both transform then predict is unnecessary and convoluted.

**C** is wrong: this defeats the purpose of the pipeline and requires maintaining separate references to the scaler and model.

**D** is wrong: calling `fit()` on the new batch would refit the scaler and model to the new data, discarding the trained pipeline.

[Snowpark ML — Pipeline](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index)

questions.stem: A data scientist has a fitted Snowpark ML Pipeline and needs to save it for later use. Select TWO valid approaches. (Select TWO)

options.text: Log the fitted pipeline to the Snowflake ML Model Registry using registry.log_model(pipeline, ...)
Call pipeline.to_bytes() and store the result in a Snowflake table column
Use Python's joblib.dump() to save the pipeline to a local file, then upload to a Snowflake internal stage
Snowpark ML pipelines cannot be persisted and must be retrained each session

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: the Snowflake ML Model Registry accepts Snowpark ML Pipeline objects just like individual estimators; log_model() serializes and stores the complete pipeline.

**C** is correct: Python's joblib serialization works for Snowpark ML models (they wrap sklearn-compatible objects). The serialized file can be uploaded to a Snowflake internal stage and later retrieved.

**B** is wrong: there is no to_bytes() method on Snowpark ML Pipeline objects.

**D** is wrong: pipelines can be persisted using both the Registry and standard Python serialization.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index

questions.stem: How does a data scientist initialize a connection to the Snowflake Feature Store in Python?

options.text: fs = FeatureStore(session=session, database='ML_DB', name='FEATURE_STORE', default_warehouse='COMPUTE_WH')
fs = FeatureStore.connect(session)
fs = session.feature_store(name='FEATURE_STORE')
fs = FeatureStore(connection_string='snowflake://...')

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: the FeatureStore constructor requires a Snowpark session, a database name, the feature store name (schema), and a default warehouse for compute.

**B** is wrong: there is no connect() class method on FeatureStore.

**C** is wrong: there is no feature_store() method on the Snowpark session object.

**D** is wrong: FeatureStore uses a Snowpark session, not a raw connection string.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index

questions.stem: Which Snowpark ML class is used to build a classification model using gradient boosted trees from the LightGBM framework, executing inside Snowflake?

options.text: snowflake.ml.modeling.lightgbm.LGBMClassifier
lightgbm.LGBMClassifier
snowflake.ml.modeling.ensemble.GradientBoostingClassifier
snowflake.ml.modeling.xgboost.XGBClassifier

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: Snowpark ML provides a LightGBM wrapper in snowflake.ml.modeling.lightgbm module as LGBMClassifier, which executes inside Snowflake.

**B** is wrong: lightgbm.LGBMClassifier is the vanilla LightGBM library that runs locally.

**C** is wrong: GradientBoostingClassifier in the ensemble module is sklearn's implementation; it is available in Snowpark ML but is a different algorithm from LightGBM.

**D** is wrong: XGBClassifier in the xgboost module is XGBoost, not LightGBM.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index

questions.stem: A data scientist calls `fs.retrieve_feature_values(spine_df, feature_views=[fv1, fv2], spine_timestamp_col='EVENT_TS')`. The spine has 1 million rows. What does point-in-time correctness guarantee here?

options.text: Features from fv1 and fv2 are joined using only feature values that were available at or before the EVENT_TS for each spine row, preventing future data leakage
Only the most recent feature values are joined, regardless of spine timestamp
Feature values are joined based on approximate timestamp matching with a 1-minute tolerance
Point-in-time correctness only applies when a single Feature View is retrieved, not multiple

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: point-in-time correct joining ensures that for each observation in the spine DataFrame, only feature values that existed at or before the EVENT_TS are used. This prevents the model from seeing future information during training, which would cause data leakage and unrealistically inflated evaluation metrics.

**B** is wrong: using only the most recent features would leak future data for historical training rows.

**C** is wrong: there is no 1-minute tolerance; the join is exact as-of the timestamp.

**D** is wrong: point-in-time correctness applies to all Feature Views in the feature_views list, not just single-view retrieval.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark-ml/feature-store/overview

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to evaluate a Snowpark ML RandomForestClassifier using 5-fold cross-validation. Which code snippet is correct?

options.text:
from snowflake.ml.modeling.model_selection import cross_val_score
scores = cross_val_score(estimator=rf_model, X=train_df, y='LABEL', cv=5)

from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
scores = cross_val_score(rf_model, train_df.to_pandas(), train_df['LABEL'].to_pandas(), cv=5)

from snowflake.ml.modeling.model_selection import cross_val_score
scores = cross_val_score(rf_model, train_df, label_cols=['LABEL'], cv=5)
scores = rf_model.cross_validate(train_df, folds=5)

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: Snowpark ML's cross_val_score (from snowflake.ml.modeling.model_selection) accepts a fitted or unfitted Snowpark ML estimator, a Snowpark DataFrame, and uses label_cols to specify the target column. cv=5 specifies 5-fold cross-validation.

**A** is wrong: the signature uses label_cols, not a separate y parameter.

**B** is wrong: using sklearn's cross_val_score with pandas conversion defeats the purpose of Snowpark ML.

**D** is wrong: cross_validate() is not a method on Snowpark ML estimator objects.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index

questions.stem:
A data scientist queries a Snowflake table using the ML Model Registry SQL invocation:

```sql
SELECT
  customer_id,
  CHURN_MODEL!PREDICT(age, tenure, monthly_spend) AS churn_score
FROM customers
WHERE region = 'WEST';
```

The model CHURN_MODEL has versions v1 (default) and v2. Which version is invoked?

options.text: v2 — the most recently logged version is always used in SQL invocations
v1 — the default version is invoked when no version qualifier is specified in the SQL syntax
Both versions — Snowflake automatically ensembles all versions for better accuracy
The query fails because a version must always be explicitly specified

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: when the SQL invocation syntax model_name!method() is used without a version qualifier, the model's designated default version is invoked. Since v1 is the default in this scenario, the SQL pipeline calls v1. This behavior enables seamless promotion — changing the default to v2 via set_default_version() or set_alias('default') would update all SQL pipelines that use the unqualified model name.

**A** is wrong: the default version is not necessarily the most recent; it is explicitly set by the team.

**C** is wrong: no automatic ensembling occurs.

**D** is wrong: a version qualifier is not required when a default version is set.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark-ml/model-registry/overview

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with a descriptive comment. Their colleague wants to read the comment for model version 'v1' of 'demand_model'. What is the correct code?

options.text: registry.get_model('demand_model').version('v1').comment
registry.get_comment('demand_model', 'v1')
registry.show_models().filter('demand_model')['comment'].iloc[0]
registry.get_model('demand_model').version('v1').get_metadata('comment')

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: the ModelVersion object exposes a comment attribute that stores the free-text description set during log_model() or subsequently updated. Accessing registry.get_model('demand_model').version('v1').comment returns the comment string.

**B** is wrong: get_comment() is not a Registry API method.

**C** is wrong: show_models() does not include per-version comments in its default output.

**D** is wrong: get_metadata('comment') is not a ModelVersion method.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark-ml/model-registry/overview

questions.stem:
A data scientist defines the following Snowpark ML model:

```python
from snowflake.ml.modeling.xgboost import XGBRegressor

model = XGBRegressor(
    input_cols=['F1','F2','F3'],
    label_cols=['PRICE'],
    output_cols=['PREDICTED_PRICE'],
    n_estimators=200,
    learning_rate=0.05
)
model.fit(train_df)
```

The training takes 45 minutes on an X-Small warehouse. What is the MOST EFFECTIVE single change to reduce training time?

options.text: Reduce n_estimators to 100
Increase the warehouse size to LARGE or X-LARGE
Convert train_df to pandas and train locally
Switch from XGBRegressor to LinearRegression

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: increasing the warehouse size provides significantly more compute (CPU, memory, and parallelism). An X-Small warehouse has 1 node; a LARGE warehouse has 8 nodes and 16x the concurrency. For Snowpark ML training on large datasets, warehouse scale-up is typically the most impactful lever.

**A** is wrong: reducing n_estimators may reduce training time but also reduces model quality; it is not the 'most effective' architectural change.

**C** is wrong: converting to pandas for local training re-introduces the data export bottleneck and was exactly what Snowpark ML was designed to avoid.

**D** is wrong: switching algorithms is a correctness and model architecture decision, not a performance optimization.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index

questions.stem: A data science team wants to understand which Feature Views contributed to training model 'churn_v3' in the ML Model Registry. Which is the correct pattern for accessing this lineage information?

options.text:
model_ref = registry.get_model('churn_model').version('v3')
dataset = model_ref.get_feature_store_dataset()
print(dataset.feature_views)
fs.get_lineage(model_name='churn_model', version='v3')
Query SNOWFLAKE.ML.FEATURE_LINEAGE WHERE MODEL='churn_model' AND VERSION='v3'
The lineage is manually documented; the Feature Store has no automated lineage API

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: when a model is logged to the Registry with a Dataset produced by the Feature Store (fs.generate_dataset() or retrieve_feature_values()), the Registry stores a reference to the Dataset, which in turn contains lineage information about which Feature Views were used. The model version object provides access to this Dataset through its metadata, enabling lineage queries.

**B** is wrong: fs.get_lineage() is not a Feature Store API method.

**C** is wrong: SNOWFLAKE.ML.FEATURE_LINEAGE is not a real system view.

**D** is wrong: the lineage is automatically tracked when the Feature Store Dataset API is used with the Registry.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark-ml/feature-store/overview

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to normalize feature values to the range [0, 1] using Snowpark ML before training. Which preprocessor should they use?

options.text: snowflake.ml.modeling.preprocessing.StandardScaler
snowflake.ml.modeling.preprocessing.RobustScaler
snowflake.ml.modeling.preprocessing.MinMaxScaler
snowflake.ml.modeling.preprocessing.MaxAbsScaler

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: `MinMaxScaler` transforms features to a [0, 1] range by computing `(x - min) / (max - min)` for each value, running the computation inside Snowflake.

**A** is wrong: `StandardScaler` standardizes to zero mean and unit variance, producing values that can be negative and are not bounded to [0, 1].

**B** is wrong: `RobustScaler` uses median and IQR for scaling, which is outlier-robust but does not produce [0, 1] range values.

**D** is wrong: `MaxAbsScaler` scales by the maximum absolute value, producing a [-1, 1] range for data with both positive and negative values.

[Snowpark ML — MinMaxScaler](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index)

questions.stem: A data scientist trains a SNOWFLAKE.ML.ANOMALY_DETECTION model using labeled data. The label column contains TRUE for known anomalies and FALSE for normal observations. After training and predicting, what columns appear in the output?

options.text: ANOMALY_SCORE only
TS (timestamp), SERIES (value), IS_ANOMALY (boolean), ANOMALY_SCORE (float), LOWER_BOUND, UPPER_BOUND
PREDICTED_LABEL and CONFIDENCE
SERIES, Z_SCORE, and OUTLIER_RANK

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: the ANOMALY_DETECTION prediction output includes the timestamp column, the original series values, IS_ANOMALY (a boolean flag), ANOMALY_SCORE (a numeric score indicating degree of anomaly), and LOWER_BOUND/UPPER_BOUND (the expected range).

**A** is wrong: multiple columns are returned, not just ANOMALY_SCORE.

**C** is wrong: PREDICTED_LABEL and CONFIDENCE are not the output column names.

**D** is wrong: Z_SCORE and OUTLIER_RANK are not the anomaly detection output columns.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/ml-functions

questions.stem: A data scientist uses `APPROX_TOP_K(product_id, 5)` on a 2-billion-row sales table. They notice one product in the result does not appear in the true top-5 when an exact GROUP BY is run. What is the expected behavior of APPROX_TOP_K?

options.text: This indicates a bug in APPROX_TOP_K; the function should always return the exact top-K items
This is expected behavior — APPROX_TOP_K uses a probabilistic algorithm that provides approximate results; items near the boundary of the top-K may occasionally be mis-ranked
APPROX_TOP_K only approximates counts; the items returned are always the exact top-K
APPROX_TOP_K errors on tables with more than 1 billion rows

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: APPROX_TOP_K is a probabilistic function. Like other approximate aggregates (APPROX_PERCENTILE, HLL), it uses a streaming algorithm (e.g., Space-Saving or Misra-Gries) that provides high-quality approximations but is not guaranteed to return the exact top-K. Items with very close frequencies near the K boundary may occasionally be mis-ranked. For the vast majority of use cases, the results are accurate enough for EDA and profiling, and the performance benefit on 2 billion rows is substantial.

**A** is wrong: approximate by design is the expected behavior; it is not a bug.

**C** is wrong: both the items and their counts are approximate.

**D** is wrong: APPROX_TOP_K has no row count limit.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to compute the Pearson correlation matrix for 50 numeric feature columns in Snowflake. What is the most efficient approach?

options.text: Collect all data to pandas with .to_pandas() and call df.corr()
Write a SQL query using CORR() aggregate for every pair of columns (50×49/2 = 1225 pairs) in a single query using conditional aggregation
Use the Snowflake CORRELATION_MATRIX() built-in function
Use Snowpark's df.stat.corr() to compute all pairwise correlations inside Snowflake without data export

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: while verbose, a single SQL query using CORR(col_i, col_j) for all pairs in one aggregation pass is the most compute-efficient approach since it requires only one full table scan. The result can then be pivoted or reshaped.

**A** is wrong: collecting 50 feature columns × millions of rows to pandas may exhaust memory and defeats the Snowflake-native approach.

**C** is wrong: CORRELATION_MATRIX() is not a built-in Snowflake SQL function.

**D** is wrong: df.stat.corr() in Snowpark only accepts two columns and computes a single pairwise correlation, not a matrix across all 50 features automatically.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index

questions.stem: A data scientist trains a SNOWFLAKE.ML.FORECAST model on weekly sales with external regressors (promotion flags and holiday indicators). How are external regressors specified during training?

options.text: They are automatically detected from extra columns in the training DataFrame beyond the timestamp and target
Using the EXOGENOUS_COLUMNS parameter in the SNOWFLAKE.ML.FORECAST stored procedure call
External regressors are not supported in SNOWFLAKE.ML.FORECAST
Using a separate SNOWFLAKE.ML.ADD_REGRESSOR() call after creating the forecast model

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: SNOWFLAKE.ML.FORECAST automatically recognizes additional columns in the training input beyond the timestamp and target value as external regressors (also called exogenous variables). These columns must also be provided for the future forecast horizon during prediction.

**B** is wrong: there is no EXOGENOUS_COLUMNS parameter; extra columns are automatically used as regressors.

**C** is wrong: external regressors are a supported feature of ML.FORECAST.

**D** is wrong: there is no ADD_REGRESSOR() function; regressors are included as columns in the training data.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/ml-functions

questions.stem: A team trains a Snowpark ML XGBClassifier and wants to explain feature importance. After calling `model.fit(train_df)`, how do they access feature importance scores?

options.text: model.feature_importances_ returns a Snowpark DataFrame with importance scores
Call model.to_sklearn().feature_importances_ to access the underlying XGBoost model's feature importance array
model.explain(train_df) generates a SHAP explanation DataFrame
Feature importance is not accessible from Snowpark ML models

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: Snowpark ML estimators expose a to_sklearn() method that returns the underlying fitted sklearn-compatible or XGBoost model. From there, standard attributes like feature_importances_ are accessible.

**A** is wrong: Snowpark ML model objects do not expose a feature_importances_ attribute directly as a Snowpark DataFrame.

**C** is wrong: SHAP explanation is not a built-in method on Snowpark ML models (though you could use the underlying model with the shap library).

**D** is wrong: feature importance is accessible via to_sklearn().

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index
